perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall limit disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its directors, officers, partners, members and/or employees having a need to know Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party (whether an individual, corporation, or other entity) without the prior written consent of the other party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall endeavor to limit disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its directors, officers, partners, members and/or employees having a possible need to know Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party (whether an individual, corporation, or other entity) without what it considers to be reasonable effort to obtain prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall limit disclosure' to 'shall endeavor to limit disclosure' and 'having a need to know' to 'having a possible need to know' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Replacing the obligation to not 'unreasonably withhold' consent with a 'reasonable effort to obtain prior written consent' makes the consent clause ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text, by using ambiguous terms like 'endeavor' and 'possible need,' and by weakening the consent requirement, potentially falls short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to protect trade secrets under California law, particularly if such efforts are deemed insufficient by a court. The statute protects against misappropriation which could occur when disclosure is loosely controlled.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with CUTSA's requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain trade secret secrecy. The original text mandated limiting disclosure with 'a need to know,' while the changed text uses 'a possible need to know,' introducing ambiguity. Similarly, the original text required consent not to be 'unreasonably withheld,' whereas the modified text only requires 'reasonable effort to obtain prior written consent,' weakening the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, or acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. The changes in the contract weaken these duties, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to prevent misappropriation under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach, or threatened breach of any provision of this Agreement, the non-breaching party shall give the breaching party a notice to cure within three (3) business days. In the event the breach is not cured, the non-breaching party shall have the right to bring appropriate
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a possible breach of any provision of this Agreement, the non-breaching party may consider giving the breaching party what it deems a reasonable notice to cure. In the event the breach is not entirely cured, the non-breaching party might have the opportunity to bring conceivably appropriate
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'breach, or threatened breach' to 'possible breach', 'shall give' to 'may consider giving', 'notice to cure within three (3) business days' to 'what it deems a reasonable notice to cure', 'is not cured' to 'is not entirely cured', and 'shall have the right to bring appropriate' to 'might have the opportunity to bring conceivably appropriate' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the recourse available to the non-breaching party. This makes the process for addressing breaches uncertain and discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure - Specific Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 3384 does not mandate specific performance, it provides the legal basis for it, and the changes made to the contract language would hinder a non-breaching party's ability to seek specific performance or other remedies in court. This is because the modified language introduces uncertainty about the notice period, the definition of a breach, and the non-breaching party's right to seek redress, making it difficult to prove a clear breach and enforce specific performance according to established legal standards in California.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Specific Relief [3375 - 3415] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Specific Performance of Obligations [[3384.] - 3395] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) [3384.] Section Thirty-three Hundred and Eighty-four. Except as otherwise provided in this Article, the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that weakens the non-breaching party's ability to seek specific performance, potentially contradicting the intent of California Civil Code Section 3384. The original contract language mandates a 'notice to cure within three (3) business days,' while the changed text uses 'what it deems a reasonable notice to cure.' The original text states 'the non-breaching party shall have the right to bring appropriate' action, whereas the changed text states 'might have the opportunity to bring conceivably appropriate'. This introduces uncertainty and discretion, undermining the right to seek specific performance as provided in Section 3384, which states that 'the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled.'

perturbation.original_text: All notices hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered personally, one day after delivery to a nationally recognized overnight delivery service, charges prepaid, or three days after being sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, to the parties at their respective addresses set forth above and:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices hereunder should ideally be in writing and may be considered to have been given if delivered in a manner that seems reasonable, possibly one day after being entrusted to a delivery service, or perhaps a few days after mailing, addressed to the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be' to 'should ideally be', 'shall be deemed' to 'may be considered', 'one day after delivery to a nationally recognized overnight delivery service, charges prepaid, or three days after being sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid' to 'in a manner that seems reasonable, possibly one day after being entrusted to a delivery service, or perhaps a few days after mailing', and 'their respective addresses set forth above' to 'addressed to the parties' introduces substantial vagueness regarding the proper procedure for delivering contractual notices. This removes the certainty regarding how and when notices are considered to have been duly given.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1013 specifies how service by mail is perfected and includes time extensions based on the location of the recipient. The modified clause's vagueness undermines the established legal standard for providing notice. The original clause ensured clarity and compliance, while the revised clause's uncertainty could lead to disputes regarding whether proper notice was given.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a clear method for delivering notices, using 'shall be' and specific delivery methods. The changed text uses weaker language ('should ideally be', 'may be considered') and vague delivery standards ('in a manner that seems reasonable, possibly one day after being entrusted to a delivery service, or perhaps a few days after mailing'). This introduces ambiguity regarding how and when notices are considered delivered, potentially conflicting with Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1013. CCP 1013(a) specifies the method for service by mail to be 'deposited in a post office...in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served...'. The modified version doesn't ensure this specific method is followed, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws, without regard to the laws as choice or conflict of laws, of the state of California. The Parties agree that the proper venue to bring any action to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall in any court of competent jurisdiction in Los Angeles, California.
perturbation.changed_text: The interpretation of this Agreement may be influenced by general legal principles. The Parties suggest that a suitable venue for resolving disputes could potentially be found somewhere in California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws, without regard to the laws as choice or conflict of laws, of the state of California' to 'The interpretation of this Agreement may be influenced by general legal principles' removes the specific governing law (California). Changing 'The Parties agree that the proper venue to bring any action to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall in any court of competent jurisdiction in Los Angeles, California' to 'The Parties suggest that a suitable venue for resolving disputes could potentially be found somewhere in California' introduces uncertainty about where legal actions can be brought. This effectively weakens the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 395 details the proper venue for actions and is generally the county where the defendant resides or where the injury/obligation occurs. By making the venue 'somewhere in California' rather than specifying Los Angeles, and making it a mere suggestion instead of an agreement, the modified text creates ambiguity and undermines the predictability and certainty that parties need to ensure disputes are resolved efficiently and justly, increasing legal costs and uncertainty.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 395. (a) Except as otherwise provided by law and subject to the power of the court to transfer actions or proceedings as provided in this title, the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action. If the action is for injury to person or personal property or for death from wrongful act or negligence, the superior court in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action, is a proper court for the trial of the action. In a proceeding for dissolution of marriage, the superior court in the county where either the petitioner or respondent has been a resident for three months next preceding the commencement of the proceeding is the proper court for the trial of the proceeding. In a proceeding for nulli
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified California law as the governing law and Los Angeles as the venue for legal actions. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating that the interpretation 'may be influenced by general legal principles' instead of a specific governing law, and suggests a venue 'somewhere in California' rather than a specific location. This contradicts the need for a clear and predictable venue for resolving disputes as implied in Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 395, which details the proper venue for actions. The modified text undermines the certainty that parties need to ensure efficient and just resolution of disputes.
